VO_EN: You haven't left the car yet.
You already know who it is.
Because you decided before you arrived.
The streetlight catches the hood of a car parked crooked at the curb.
You look at the address again.
Zip code tells you everything the dispatcher didn't.
You don't protect people. You sort them.
They teach you a sentence before they teach you to aim.
You learn to say it the way you learn to field-strip a weapon.
The instructor writes it on the board.
Thirty recruits in the room and nobody asks when to use it.
The instructor explains when not to.
The holster pulls at your hip.
You walk slower because of it.
Every step feels heavier on the left side where the holster hangs.
You listen to it too carefully.
You hope you never have to use it.
But on the way back to the car your hand drifts to the holster.
You don't know why it moved.
First time you draw on someone.
Domestic call. Man in the doorway.
You know that before you pull it.
The dog behind the chain-link stops barking.
The woman in the doorway covers her mouth but doesn't make a sound.
What comes instead is something else.
You hold the position longer than you need to.
Because putting it away feels like giving something back.
You say each word like you're reading from a card.
Your hand is on the holster again.
The camera blinks red on your chest.
The same stillness in your hands.
You try to remember if you checked whether they were armed first.
You stop waiting for them to stop.
You stop waiting for them to understand.
The commands come out before you've finished deciding what to say.
'Don't move.' 'Get down.' 'Hands where I can see them.'
All three land in the same breath.
'Hands up. On the ground. Don't move.'
You shout 'drop the weapon' after the shot.
The audio doesn't have timestamps in court.
One of them has a pen that clicks.
He clicks it seven times before he stops.
They clear you in forty minutes.
You write 'furtive movement' in the report.
You don't remember when the first time was.
Your partner saw what you wrote.
Your shooting count is the highest in the precinct.
They don't call it a problem.
You drive through the neighborhood off duty once.
The words come out before the question finishes.
You don't think about them anymore.
They come out the way a cough does.
You try to remember the last time you meant them.
You're not sure when that started.
The ID is on the ground.
It was already in their hand.
You told them to reach for it.
You don't remember deciding to fire.
The rookie is standing behind you.
It's the same look you had once.
In the training room. Before you learned the five words.
You don't remember when you lost it.
You teach the rookie the five words.
They sit across from you at the desk.
The fluorescent light above you hums.
You don't know how long it's been doing that.
But it sounds wrong in their voice.
Like hearing your own voice on a recording.
You try to remember the first time you said it and meant it.
You look at the report on your desk.
You don't recognize the person they describe.
There's an ID photo stapled to the corner of the file.
You don't remember seeing that face.
You write it at the bottom of the page.
You don't remember if you did.
Your hand is on the holster before the dispatcher finishes talking.
You haven't left the car yet.
